sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['M', 'W', 'G'] and n['pixels'] < 20:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(r for r,c in b), max(r for r,c in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(c for r,c in b), max(c for r,c in b)
        print(f"color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} center=({(r_min+r_max)//2},{(c_min+c_max)//2})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The magenta moved from (40, 33) to (30, 36) - it moved UP toward the dark gray at the ring! And the white moved from (59, 27) to (40, 33) - it followed the magenta's old position. So when I swapped white and dark gray, the direction of movement reversed! The magenta now moves toward the dark gray square.
So the goal is clear now: the magenta always moves toward the dark gray square. By default, the dark gray is at the end of the path (bottom), so magenta moves down. But I swapped them, so now dark gray is at the ring (top) and magenta moves up.
But wait - in the original configuration, the magenta was already moving toward the dark gray (which was at the bottom). So the magenta naturally moves toward the dark gray.
Now the magenta is at (30, 36), moving toward the dark gray at (21, 39). I need to keep advancing until the magenta reaches the dark gray square. Let me continue.
